full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If my grandfather, Gus, were here, God rest his soul, he would surely ask, what's a nice<|10.70|> <|10.70|> Irish girl like you doing in a place like this?<|13.62|> <|15.76|> Literally surrounded by Scots.<|18.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.22|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.94|> There's a lot of myth around this particular Sunday, this service, Kirk and O'the Tartan,<|7.82|> <|7.96|> how it began and why.<|9.46|> <|10.66|> My favorite commentary on it was by a Scotsman who said it was pure flummery to think Presbyterians<|19.02|> <|19.02|> would ever bless inanimate objects, especially in the 1700s.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so this explains why you've been coming to us for the past 41 years.<|4.40|> <|8.72|> Like my Irish Catholic ancestors, like the Scots, like the American Episcopal Church,<|17.76|> <|18.40|> we've all had our share of problems with the English.<|21.54|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.16|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we Episcopalians owe much to the ancestors of you in this kilted throng.<|7.88|> <|8.96|> You see, there's good reason for the Scottish flag appearing in the upper left corner of the Episcopal shield.<|16.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> History tells us that on November 14, 1784, just a year after the official end of the<|9.42|> <|9.42|> Revolutionary War, Samuel Seabury was consecrated in Scotland as the first bishop in the new<|18.34|> <|18.34|> Protestant Episcopal Church in the new country.<|22.40|> <|22.40|> Amen.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While he had been sent by the people of Connecticut to England to seek consecration in the Mother Church, the Church of England,<|10.02|> <|10.58|> the Church of England refused this consecration since Seabury could not and would not swear allegiance as required to the king.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The same king against whom the colonists had fought a long and bloody war to gain their independence.<|8.14|> <|9.68|> So Seabury did the only natural next step.<|12.46|> <|12.60|> He moved north to Scotland, where the Anglican church was not connected to the crown as the established church.<|20.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and where there were bishops willing to do the deed, and who had the credentials to do it,<|6.70|> <|7.02|> so that Seabury would be part of the apostolic succession.<|11.16|> <|12.54|> Indeed, we owe our very beginnings to you lot.<|16.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so we thank you for the origin of our bishops and Sean Connery.<|5.70|> <|7.92|> And we thank you for the perpetuation and the protection of that gene that guarantees red hair.<|13.94|> <|14.84|> For Sir Arthur Conan Doyle.<|16.80|> <|17.34|> For Robert Burns' sentimental Auld Lang Syne.<|21.38|> <|22.22|> And poetic, My Love is Like a Red, Red Rose.<|24.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A poem that is remarkably and shockingly more famous than his address to a haggis.<|7.76|> <|9.68|> And did I thank you for Sean Connery?<|12.16|> <|14.56|> You Scots are a proud people, and it is indeed a proud heritage.<|21.08|> <|21.08|> Thank you.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We spend a great deal of time trying to anchor ourselves, don't we?<|7.74|> <|8.58|> Tethering ourselves to a time, a place, a culture, a clan, a name, a nationality, to understand who we are.<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are rarely just American.<|3.40|> <|4.14|> We are almost always hyphenated Americans.<|8.12|> <|8.92|> Irish American, Scottish American, rarely just American.<|14.86|> <|14.86|> American.<|15.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I remember that I oddly felt my most American when we lived overseas in Sweden for two years.<|8.94|> <|9.66|> I had finally an identity that was clear and simple.<|13.78|> <|14.36|> No hyphenation.<|15.24|> <|16.24|> We long to know and proclaim who we are.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Wired Magazine reported this past December that between Black Friday and Cyber Monday,<|8.00|> <|8.00|> leading personal genomics company AncestryDNA sold over 1.5 million testing kits designed<|20.00|> <|20.00|> to provide insights into our ethnicity and familial connections.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And they went on to contextualize that by saying, that's like 2,000 gallons of saliva.<|8.08|> <|9.44|> Enough to fill a modest, above-ground swimming pool with the genetic history of every person in the city of Philadelphia.<|20.08|> <|20.08|> Okay.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> DNA testing was the hot Christmas gift in 2017.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> And we are no longer just content to know our own roots, no longer happy with saying we have a mutt.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> We now want to be able to say that Fido is part Malamute, part Boxer, and part Dachshund.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes, DNA testing for dogs is all the rage.<|4.34|> <|5.06|> And yes, we will not only bless your tartans today, bring your dogs in October and we'll<|10.42|> <|10.42|> bless them too.<|11.36|> <|13.50|> My friends, we seem to be living in times desperate to know where we belong and exactly<|23.00|> <|23.00|> who we are.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And then we are given the gift of this morning's gospel.<|5.54|> <|6.30|> How reassuring in all of our doubt to hear the final refrain of John's text full of I am statements.<|18.28|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I am the bread of life.<|3.26|> <|3.86|> I am the light of the world.<|6.52|> <|7.32|> I am the gate.<|9.46|> <|10.16|> I am the good shepherd.<|12.44|> <|13.12|> I am the resurrection and the life.<|17.38|> <|17.94|> I am the way, the truth, and the life.<|22.48|> <|23.40|> I am the vine.<|24.90|> <|25.50|> And you are the branches.<|26.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|>.<|1.24|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have an identity.<|3.00|> <|4.00|> When we are uncertain of who we are, we need only to remember this.<|10.20|> <|11.02|> We are branches of the true vine.<|13.78|> <|14.70|> We are planted by the vine grower and attached to the vine.<|19.70|> <|19.70|> We are branches of the true vine.<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through our attachment, our kinship with the vine, we are fruitful.<|6.66|> <|7.78|> We have purpose.<|8.78|> <|9.30|> We have connection.<|10.56|> <|11.30|> We have a sense of belonging.<|13.58|> <|14.40|> We have community.<|15.74|> <|16.58|> We have identity.<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> presiding Bishop Michael Curry proclaims that we are the Episcopal branch of the Jesus movement.<|8.22|> <|8.66|> There's a reason he said branch, not arm, not division, not wing, not section.<|16.72|> <|16.72|> We are the Episcopal branch attached to the vine planted by the vine grower.<|27.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our Episcopal identity is in Christ.<|5.04|> <|5.76|> Our identity, our heritage, our present, and our future.<|13.24|> <|14.40|> A branch of the vine.<|16.18|> <|16.96|> A branch called upon to produce fruit.<|20.76|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.24|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.56|> The imperative to bear fruit appears six times in this very brief text.<|7.96|> <|8.98|> And fruit bearing is not something that we can force to happen.<|12.88|> <|13.00|> We don't just pop it out.<|14.34|> <|15.40|> The fruit grows because the vine grower has tended the vine.<|20.76|> <|21.34|> The vine is strong and true fruit.<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and we as branches are securely attached and receiving all that is necessary for us to bear fruit.<|9.40|> <|10.54|> The closer our relationship with Jesus, the more closely we identify with him,<|18.14|> <|18.82|> the more we know who and whose we are.<|23.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> This passage from John is particularly precious to me as canon pastor here at the cathedral.<|9.46|> <|10.66|> It is one of the readings in the section of our book of common prayer called Communion Under Special Circumstances.<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is the liturgy for sharing the Eucharist with those who cannot be in community with us on Sundays.<|7.60|> <|7.60|> The rubrics say this,<|10.60|> <|11.80|> It is desirable that fellow parishioners, relatives, and friends be present when possible to communicate with them.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A Eucharistic visitor or I will bring community to you.<|6.44|> <|7.50|> We will remind you where you belong.<|11.08|> <|12.22|> When I visit one of you in the hospital, at home, in a nursing home or a rehab center,<|20.36|> <|20.84|> I read this text.<|23.24|> <|23.24|> I read this text.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus said,<|1.62|> <|2.46|> "'Abide in me as I in you.'"<|6.78|> <|6.78|> As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself unless it abides in the vine,<|11.48|> <|11.48|> neither can you unless you abide in me.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I am the vine, you are the branches.<|4.12|> <|5.16|> By this my Father is glorified, that you bear much fruit and so prove to be my disciples.<|13.06|> <|13.06|> As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you.<|18.78|> <|19.92|> Abide in my love.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have our identity in community with one another.<|5.18|> <|6.06|> All branches connected to the same vine,<|9.76|> <|10.18|> planted by the same vine grower.<|13.08|> <|14.08|> When we greet each other at the peace of the world,<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> talk over coffee after the service, share a meal at Cathedral Night, work together on Love Denver Day,<|8.84|> <|9.96|> kneel together at the altar rail, or carry communion to those who cannot be here with us on a Sunday.<|17.84|> <|18.72|> We are abiding, and we are bearing fruit.<|23.14|> <|23.14|> Amen.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And my friends, we have an identity.<|3.86|> <|4.64|> Not one that will ever be detected on any home DNA test,<|9.96|> <|10.44|> but one that is nevertheless true and sure and abiding.<|17.18|> <|18.08|> We have our truest sense of self,<|21.62|> <|22.42|> our deepest identity in the great I am.<|27.26|> <|27.26|> Amen.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are the branches, and he is the vine.<|4.26|><|endoftext|>
